title: Why I Chose Spring Boot for My School Management System
summary: Spring Boot made it easy to build a full REST API fast. Here's what I learned along the way.
content:
When I started building the School Management System, I needed a framework that could handle REST APIs, database connections, and authentication without too much boilerplate. Spring Boot was the answer.

The auto-configuration saved me hours of setup. With just a few annotations like @RestController and @Entity, I had a working API connected to MySQL in under an hour.

One challenge I faced was handling relationships between students, classes, and teachers. JPA made this manageable with @OneToMany and @ManyToOne, but I had to be careful with lazy loading to avoid N+1 query problems.

My biggest lesson: always plan your database schema before writing any code. Changing relationships mid-project is painful.

Next up, I want to add JWT authentication so only authorized users can access the admin dashboard.

title: Building My First Python Django App
summary: From zero Django knowledge to a working hotel management app in two weeks. Here's how I did it.
content:
Two weeks ago I had never written a single line of Django. Today I have a working hotel management app. Here's what the journey looked like.

Day 1-3: I spent the first three days just reading the Django documentation and following the official tutorial. Django's 'batteries included' philosophy means a lot is already built for you — ORM, admin panel, authentication.

Day 4-7: I started building the actual models — Room, Guest, Booking, Invoice. The Django admin panel was incredibly useful for testing my models without building a frontend first.

Day 8-11: Built the views and templates. Django's template language is simple but powerful enough for what I needed.

Day 12-14: Added Bootstrap for styling and deployed to a local server for testing.

The biggest surprise? Django's built-in admin panel. For internal tools, you almost don't need to build a frontend at all. It's that powerful.

What's next: I want to add email notifications when a booking is confirmed.

title: Hello World — Why I Started This Blog
summary: A short intro about who I am, why I code, and what I hope to share here.
content:
My name is Krai Theara. I'm a web development student in Phnom Penh, Cambodia.

I started this blog because I want to document my learning journey as I build real projects. Too many tutorials show the finished product — I want to show the messy middle: the bugs, the wrong turns, and the moments when something finally clicks.

Why do I code? Simple: I want to build technology that helps people in my community. Cambodia is growing fast. There are schools without digital systems, hotels using paper ledgers, and communities with no online presence. I want to change that, one project at a time.

What will I write about? Mostly what I'm learning — Java, Spring Boot, Python, JavaScript, databases, APIs. But also the bigger picture: why certain tools exist, how to think about software design, and what it's like to be a self-taught developer in Southeast Asia.

If you're on a similar journey, I hope this helps. Let's build something great.
